agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle class constraints, and configurable stop duration. This feature spans multiple bounded contexts and encodes both hard physical constraints and soft operational policies.",
    "bounded_context": "This feature touches at least four bounded contexts: Route Planning (optimization, recalculation), Fleet Management (driver shifts, vehicle class licensing), Delivery Management (delivery lifecycle, zone assignment), and Customer Notification (delivery proximity alerts). The product description conflates them, which is a domain drift risk.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period for a driver, beginning when they clock in and ending when they clock out or reach max hours. A shift is the lifecycle anchor for route assignment." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic partition of the service area used to scope delivery assignment and route optimization. Each zone may have its own operational overrides (e.g., stop duration)." },
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated to minimize travel time/distance within a driver's zone for their current shift." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been accepted into the system but not yet assigned to a driver's route, or assigned but not yet attempted." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that did not result in successful handoff to the recipient. Triggers route recalculation as the stop is either retried, deferred, or returned." },
      { "term": "Route Deviation", "definition": "When a driver's actual GPS position or stop sequence diverges from the suggested optimized route beyond a defined threshold." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "A rolling proximity notification boundary: the three upcoming stops on a driver's active route. Customers at these stops receive a notification." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "The category of vehicle a driver is authorized to operate (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Determines which deliveries they may be assigned based on shipment requirements." },
      { "term": "Stop Duration", "definition": "The estimated time a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes but is overridable per zone by the operations team." },
      { "term": "Recalculation Trigger", "definition": "An event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces recomputation: new delivery in zone, failed delivery, or driver deviation." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A driver MUST only be assigned deliveries whose shipment requirements are compatible with their licensed vehicle class.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a physical and legal constraint. A motorcycle cannot carry freight. Violating this means undeliverable assignments and potential regulatory/insurance violations. No VP can override physics or law.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D assigned to driver X: D.requiredVehicleClass ∈ X.licensedVehicleClasses"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver MUST only be assigned deliveries within their assigned zone during their active shift.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone-scoping is a system integrity constraint for route optimization. Cross-zone assignments corrupt the optimization model and create conflicting ownership of deliveries.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D on driver X's route: D.zone == X.currentShift.zone"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When a driver starts their shift, the system automatically generates an optimized route from all pending deliveries in their zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational policy. The business could decide to allow manual route creation, or defer optimization until a threshold of deliveries is met. The trigger ('shift start') is a business decision, not a physical law.",
      "testable_assertion": "Within T seconds of a driver's shift starting, a route exists for that driver containing all eligible pending deliveries in their zone."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a new delivery is added to the driver's zone, a delivery on the route is marked as failed, or the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "These are operational recalculation triggers. The business could add or remove triggers (e.g., recalculate on traffic changes, or batch new deliveries instead of recalculating immediately). Policy, not physics.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an active route for driver X: (a) adding a pending delivery to X's zone triggers recalculation, (b) marking a stop as failed triggers recalculation, (c) detecting driver deviation triggers recalculation."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Customers are notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The notification window size (3) is a business preference. It could be changed to 2 or 5. The notification itself is a UX policy, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a route is active and delivery D is at position N where N <= currentStopIndex + 3, and D's customer has not already been notified for this route version, a notification is dispatched to D's customer."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop duration is 4 minutes per delivery stop.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational default. The spec explicitly states it can be overridden per zone.",
      "testable_assertion": "When no zone-level override exists, the route optimizer uses 4 minutes as the stop duration for ETA calculations."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The operations team can override the estimated stop duration on a per-zone basis.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an administrative policy lever. The override authority and granularity (per-zone) are business decisions.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a zone Z has a stop duration override of N minutes, the route optimizer uses N minutes (not the default 4) for all stops in zone Z."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "A route recalculation MUST NOT re-sequence stops that have already been completed.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Completed deliveries are historical facts. Reordering them would corrupt delivery records, timestamps, and customer-facing tracking data. This is data integrity.",
      "testable_assertion": "After recalculation, all stops marked 'completed' retain their original position and completion timestamp; only pending/future stops may be reordered."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "A customer MUST NOT receive duplicate 'next 3 stops' notifications for the same delivery within a single route execution, even if recalculation re-enters them into the window.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a UX quality rule. Duplicate notifications erode trust but don't corrupt system state. The business could decide re-notification is acceptable after a delay.",
      "testable_assertion": "For a given delivery D and route execution R, at most one 'next 3 stops' notification is sent to D's customer."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are 12 pending deliveries in their zone compatible with their vehicle class",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "An optimized route is generated containing exactly those 12 deliveries, ordered to minimize travel time/distance"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops",
      "when": "A new pending delivery is added to their zone that is compatible with their vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery among the remaining stops, preserving all completed stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and marks stop #4 as a failed delivery",
      "when": "The failure is recorded",
      "then": "The route is recalculated with the failed delivery either removed or repositioned per failed-delivery policy, and remaining pending stops are re-optimized"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver deviates from the suggested route (e.g., skips stop #3 and drives toward stop #5)",
      "when": "The system detects the deviation exceeds the configured threshold",
      "then": "The route is recalculated from the driver's current position, preserving completed stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver's active route has stops [A, B, C, D, E, F] and the driver just completed stop A",
      "when": "The route advances to stop B as current",
      "then": "Customers for stops B, C, and D receive 'next 3 stops' notifications (if not already notified)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A motorcycle-licensed driver in Zone 5",
      "when": "Route generation runs and Zone 5 has pending deliveries including 2 freight shipments",
      "then": "The freight shipments are excluded from the driver's route; only motorcycle-compatible deliveries are assigned"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "Zone 7 has a stop duration override of 6 minutes set by the operations team",
      "when": "A route is generated for a driver in Zone 7",
      "then": "All ETA calculations use 6 minutes per stop, not the default 4 minutes"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A route recalculation occurs and delivery D re-enters the 'next 3 stops' window",
      "when": "D's customer was already notified during this route execution",
      "then": "No duplicate notification is sent to D's customer"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift in Zone 3 but there are zero pending deliveries in Zone 3",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "No route is generated; the driver enters an idle/awaiting-deliveries state"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-10",
      "given": "Two drivers start shifts simultaneously in the same zone with 20 pending deliveries",
      "when": "Both shifts activate",
      "then": "Each delivery is assigned to exactly one driver; no delivery appears on both routes"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Driver starts shift but all pending deliveries in their zone require a vehicle class they are not licensed for", "expected_behavior": "No route is generated. Driver enters idle state. System should alert operations that there is a vehicle class mismatch requiring dispatcher intervention." },
    { "scenario": "Route recalculation is triggered while a previous recalculation is still in progress", "expected_behavior": "The in-progress recalculation is cancelled or the triggers are queued/debounced. The system must not produce two competing routes for the same driver." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is added to a zone where multiple drivers have active routes", "expected_behavior": "The new delivery is assigned to exactly one driver (based on proximity, capacity, or load-balancing logic). The spec does not define multi-driver arbitration — this needs clarification." },
    { "scenario": "Driver completes all stops on their route mid-shift", "expected_behavior": "Driver enters idle/awaiting-deliveries state within their shift. If new deliveries arrive in their zone, a fresh route is generated." },
    { "scenario": "Customer notification fails (unreachable phone, invalid email)", "expected_behavior": "The notification failure must not block route progression. Failed notifications should be logged for retry or operations review." },
    { "scenario": "Operations team sets stop duration override to 0 or a negative value", "expected_behavior": "The system must validate the override: stop duration must be a positive value. Reject invalid overrides with a clear error." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is cancelled by the customer while it is in the 'next 3 stops' window", "expected_behavior": "The delivery is removed from the route, triggering recalculation. The customer receives a cancellation confirmation rather than a delivery notification." },
    { "scenario": "Driver's GPS signal is lost during an active route", "expected_behavior": "The system cannot detect deviation. Route remains as-is. Deviation detection resumes when GPS signal returns. No phantom recalculations." },
    { "scenario": "A zone boundary is redrawn while a driver has an active route", "expected_behavior": "Existing routes should complete under their original zone assignment. New deliveries are assigned based on the updated zone boundaries." },
    { "scenario": "The same driver ends a shift and starts a new one in a different zone", "expected_behavior": "The previous route is finalized (incomplete stops handled per policy). A new route is generated in the new zone. 'Next 3 stops' notification tracking resets." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Optimized routing reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries per shift (higher revenue throughput), and reduces failed delivery costs through dynamic recalculation. Incorrect vehicle class assignment could result in insurance claims and regulatory fines. Stop duration accuracy directly affects delivery promise reliability — broken promises drive customer churn.",
    "operational": "This feature introduces significant real-time computation load (route optimization is NP-hard, requiring heuristic solvers). Recalculation frequency must be tuned to avoid thrashing. Operations team gains a new lever (stop duration overrides) requiring training and governance. Multi-driver zone arbitration will be a daily dispatcher concern.",
    "user": "Drivers get clear guidance, reducing cognitive load and navigation errors. Customers gain transparency via the 'next 3 stops' notification — this sets delivery time expectations and reduces 'where is my package' support calls. However, frequent recalculations may confuse drivers if the route changes mid-transit. Notification timing must feel helpful, not spammy.",
    "risk": "High risk of race conditions in multi-driver zones (double-assignment of deliveries). GPS dependency for deviation detection creates a reliability concern. Route optimization algorithm quality directly impacts all downstream metrics — a poor optimizer negates the entire feature's value. The spec is silent on what happens to failed deliveries after recalculation (retry? return to depot? re-queue?) — this ambiguity is a significant implementation risk.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Primary users. Gain automated route guidance. Risk of frustration from frequent recalculations disrupting their flow." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Receive proactive delivery notifications. Expectations are now set — if ETAs are inaccurate due to bad stop duration estimates, trust erodes." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gains zone-level configuration power (stop duration overrides). Must monitor zone-driver-vehicle class alignment. New operational burden." },
      { "group": "Dispatchers", "impact": "Partially displaced by automation. Still needed for edge cases (vehicle mismatch, multi-driver arbitration, failed delivery disposition). Role shifts from manual routing to exception handling." },
      { "group": "Finance/Business", "impact": "Expects measurable improvement in deliveries-per-shift and fuel cost reduction. Needs KPIs defined before launch." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec has several significant ambiguities: (1) no definition of what happens to failed deliveries after recalculation, (2) no multi-driver arbitration logic for shared zones, (3) no deviation detection threshold defined, (4) no clarification on whether route optimization considers real-time traffic or is purely distance-based, (5) no definition of delivery-to-vehicle-class mapping semantics. These gaps affect core business logic and could lead to materially different implementations.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class constraint is a hard invariant — clear and unambiguous",
      "Recalculation triggers are well-enumerated",
      "Stop duration default and override mechanism is clearly specified",
      "Customer notification window concept is clear"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Failed delivery disposition after recalculation (retry, return, defer?)",
      "Multi-driver zone arbitration — how is a new delivery assigned when multiple drivers are active?",
      "Deviation detection threshold — what constitutes a 'deviation'?",
      "Whether optimization considers traffic, time windows, or driver preferences",
      "Notification channel and retry semantics"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift",
      "Vehicle class compatibility is a property of both the delivery (required class) and the driver (licensed classes), not just the vehicle currently driven",
      "Route optimization is intra-zone only — no cross-zone routing",
      "Completed stops are immutable facts that survive recalculation",
      "The 'next 3 stops' window is based on route sequence position, not geographic proximity"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not define what happens to a failed delivery after route recalculation. Is it retried later in the same route? Returned to the depot? Deferred to the next shift? Re-queued as a new pending delivery? This fundamentally changes recalculation behavior.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Customers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A) Failed delivery is re-queued as pending and may re-enter the same or another driver's route",
        "B) Failed delivery is removed from routing entirely and escalated to operations for manual disposition",
        "C) Failed delivery is retried once at the end of the current route, then escalated if it fails again"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C — retry-then-escalate is standard in last-mile logistics and balances delivery success rates with driver efficiency."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "When multiple drivers have active routes in the same zone and a new delivery arrives, the spec says 'the route must be recalculated' but does not specify which driver gets the new delivery. This is a critical arbitration gap.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Dispatchers", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A) Assign to the driver with the fewest remaining stops (load balancing)",
        "B) Assign to the driver whose current position is geographically closest to the new delivery",
        "C) Assign to the driver whose route would have the least total distance increase",
        "D) Require dispatcher manual assignment"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C — least-cost insertion is the most common approach in route optimization literature and produces the best overall efficiency."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No threshold or definition is provided for what constitutes a 'driver deviation' from the suggested route. Is it a distance threshold? A skipped stop? A time-based divergence? Without this, deviation detection is unimplementable.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A) Distance-based: driver is more than X meters from the expected route path",
        "B) Sequence-based: driver skips a stop and proceeds to a later one",
        "C) Both: either condition triggers recalculation"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C — both conditions indicate the route is no longer optimal. Sequence deviation is simpler to detect reliably; distance deviation handles mid-segment rerouting."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The spec does not address whether the route optimization algorithm should consider real-time traffic data, delivery time windows (customer availability), package priority levels, or driver break requirements. These factors materially change route quality and business outcomes.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Customers", "Finance/Business"],
      "options": [
        "A) V1 uses distance-only optimization; traffic and time windows are deferred to V2",
        "B) V1 includes traffic data and customer time windows as optimization constraints"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A for initial release — distance-only is predictable and testable. Layer in traffic and time windows after validating the core recalculation mechanics work correctly."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
